question_text: Two liquids, A and B, are mixed together in a container. Liquid A has a density of 0.8 g/cm³ and Liquid B has a density of 1.2 g/cm³. Why do these liquids not mix uniformly, and which liquid would be found at the top of the container?
correct_answer: The liquids separate because they have different densities; Liquid A (0.8 g/cm³) would float on top because it is less dense than Liquid B (1.2 g/cm³)
explanation: Liquids with different densities do not fully mix and form separate layers—the less dense liquid floats on top of the denser one. Since Liquid A is less dense (0.8 g/cm³), it rises to the top, while the denser Liquid B (1.2 g/cm³) sinks to the bottom.
